coredata.dc:title: High photoluminescent TPA and the ratiometric sensor for free chlorine
coredata.dc:creator: Fang, Qingqing
Wang, Qian
Lin, Wei
You, Xu
Dong, Yongqiang
Chi, Yuwu
coredata.dc:description: A facile and high yield method is introduced to synthesize 3,5-dihydro-5-oxo-2H-thiazolo[3,2-a]pyridine-3,7-dicarboxylic acid (TPA) through the “one-step” condensation reaction of citric acid and l-cysteine. For the first time, the produced TPA molecules are purified by the simple crystallization, and the photoluminescence (PL) properties of the TPA are investigated. The obtained purified TPA shows bright blue PL, with a PL quantum yield as high as 90.5%. The sulfur atom of TPA molecule can be selectively oxidized by free chlorine into sulfoxide, which is a strong electron withdrawing group. As a result, the conjugation is decreased, leading to a great PL quenching. On the basis, a sensitive and selective PL sensor is developed for the detection of free chlorine. Furthermore, tris(2,2-bipyridine)ruthenium(II) chloride hexahydrate, whose red PL signal is inert to the free chlorine, can be used to provide a background PL signal. Thus, a visual PL ratiometric sensor is developed, which is convenient for the on-site and on-line detection of free chlorine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 3,5-Dihydro-5-oxo-2H-thiazolo[3,2-a]pyridine-3,7-dicarboxylic acid
Synthesis
Photoluminescence
Free chlorine
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Principle of the PL sensor for free chlorine based on TPA
Establishment of the PL sensing for free chlorine

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Basic Research Program of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of TPA from the condensation reaction between citric acid and l-cysteine.
Reaction mechanism of TPA towards free chlorine.
Mass spectra of TPA (a) and the oxidation product (b).
UV and PL (Ex: 345nm) spectra of 10μM TPA solution in the absence and presence of 14μM free chlorine (pH=4). The inset shows the corresponding PL photos under the irradiation of 365nm UV light.
Effect of pH value on the PL response of 10μM TPA in the absence and presence of 10μM NaClO, and the corresponding pH-dependent quenching efficiency (reaction time: 20min, Ex: 345nm, Em: 415nm).

Selectivity of the TPA-based sensor for free chlorine over other ions (pH: 4, reaction time: 20min, Ex: 345nm, Em: 415nm). The concentration of TPA is 10μM. All the interference ions are 100μM, except for MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2− are 10μM.

PL intensities of 10μM TPA upon addition of various concentrations of NaClO (from top to down: 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14μM, pH: 4, reaction time: 20min, Ex: 345nm). Inset: Stern-Volmer plot of PL quenching efficiency of the TPA by free chlorine.
Visual PL photos of the mixed solution (10μM TPA+28μM Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O) added with various concentrations of free chlorine under 365nm UV light.

head.ce:title: High photoluminescent TPA and the ratiometric sensor for free chlorine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, and Department of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fujian, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety
Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A high photoluminescent pyridine-derivative, 3,5-dihydro-5-oxo-2H-thiazolo[3,2-a]pyridine-3,7-dicarboxylic acid, was synthesized and used as the probe for free chlorine detection.

A facile and high yield method is introduced to synthesize 3,5-dihydro-5-oxo-2H-thiazolo[3,2-a]pyridine-3,7-dicarboxylic acid (TPA) through the “one-step” condensation reaction of citric acid and l-cysteine. For the first time, the produced TPA molecules are purified by the simple crystallization, and the photoluminescence (PL) properties of the TPA are investigated. The obtained purified TPA shows bright blue PL, with a PL quantum yield as high as 90.5%. The sulfur atom of TPA molecule can be selectively oxidized by free chlorine into sulfoxide, which is a strong electron withdrawing group. As a result, the conjugation is decreased, leading to a great PL quenching. On the basis, a sensitive and selective PL sensor is developed for the detection of free chlorine. Furthermore, tris(2,2-bipyridine)ruthenium(II) chloride hexahydrate, whose red PL signal is inert to the free chlorine, can be used to provide a background PL signal. Thus, a visual PL ratiometric sensor is developed, which is convenient for the on-site and on-line detection of free chlorine.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, citric acid (CA) coupled with some amino groups containing molecules have been well used in high photoluminescent (PL) materials, including polymers and carbon based dots (CDs) [1–4]. It was proposed that some high PL pyridine-derivatives are produced as by-products during the preparation of these materials [1,2]. However, the pyridine-derivatives are usually difficult to be separated from the materials, usually using the high efficiency liquid chromatography and chromatography [1,2]. Therefore, the pure pyridine-derivatives are difficult to be largely obtained. As a result, the PL properties of the molecules can not be studied in detail, which is unfavorable for studying the roles of these pyridine-derivatives in the PL of CDs and polymers. What’s worse, the applications of these pyridine-derivatives are limited. Herein, we develop a facile method to synthesize a blue emissive molecule, 3,5-dihydro-5-oxo-2H-thiazolo[3,2-a]pyridine-3,7-dicarboxylic acid (TPA), using CA and l-cysteine. The as-synthesized TPA can be easily purified by crystallization. Then, the luminescent properties of the TPA are investigated for the first time. The obtained purified TPA molecule exhibits bright blue emission in aqueous solution, with a PL quantum yield as high as 90.5%, which is comparable with those of the well-known blue emissive fluorine and fluorine derivatives [5]. Furthermore, the TPA shows good solubility in aqueous solution. The results indicate that the TPA is an excellent blue PL phosphor, which can be applied in sensing. For instance, the TPA can act as an excellent PL probe for the detection of free chlorine.

Free chlorine, including chlorine, hypochlorous acid and hypochlorite, are widely used as a disinfectant and bleaching agent in water treatment [6]. Usually, the concentration of free chlorine in the treated water needs to be controlled strictly, making sure that the pathogenic bacteria and viruses in the water can be killed effectively while the residual free chlorine level is not too high to produce any negative side effect [7–9]. Accordingly, monitoring the concentration of free chlorine in water is quite necessary. Besides the most popular colorimetry method based on NN-diethyl-p-phenylenediamine (DPD) [10], many efforts have been made to develop alternative detection methods based on different principles, including chemiluminescence [11–14], electrochemical [15–18] and liquid chromatography [19,20]. In particular, the PL methods based on various nanomaterials usually show many advantages [21–28], such as good selectivity and sensitivity, fast response and low cost. However, these nanomaterials are usually inconvenient to be quantified, leading to serious difficulties in popularizing these methods. In this paper, it is found that the blue PL emission of TPA can be selectively quenched by the strongly oxidative free chlorine. After investigating the quenching mechanism and optimizing the experimental conditions, a sensitive and selective PL sensor is developed for free chlorine using TPA as the PL probe. Furthermore, tris(2,2-bipyridine)ruthenium(II) chloride hexahydrate (Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O), whose red PL signal is inert to the free chlorine, is used to provide a background signal. As a result, a sensitive and visual PL ratiometric sensing system for the detection of residual free chlorine is developed, which is quite convenient for the on-site and on-line detection.

CA (99+%), l-cysteine(97.0%) and Ru(bpy)3Cl2•6H2O (98%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. A 10% sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) standardized by iodometric titration was used for daily preparation of diluted working standards. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solutions of different pH values were prepared by titrating 0.1molL−1 phosphoric acid solution with a concentrated sodium hydroxide solution (2molL−1) to the required pH values. Other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. Doubly distilled (DI) water was used throughout the experiment.

As shown in Scheme 1 , the TPA is synthesized through the condensation reaction between CA and l-cysteine [2]. In a typical experiment, 5.0g citric acid and 2.9g l-cysteine were put in a 100mL beaker and dissolved in 10mL DI water, then dried at 70°C for 12h to form a yellow slurry. Subsequently, the slurry was dissolved with 10mL concentrated H2SO4 and heated at 120°C for 3h. Finally, the reaction was terminated by adding 50mL DI water. The resulted solution was placed quietly for 2day at room temperature. A lot of yellow crystals were formed at the bottom of the beaker. After a thorough wash with DI water, the crystals were collected and dried at 60°C. About 1.6g TPA crystals were finally obtained.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was measured on an FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet 360). UV−vis absorption spectra were characterized by a UV/vis/NIR spectrophotometer (Lambda 750). All PL spectra were obtained by a FL spectrophotometer (F 4600). Mass spectra of TPA and the oxidation product were measured using an Exactive Plus mass spectrometer.

A local tap water sample was collected and used to evaluate the operability of the developed sensor. Aliquots (500μL) of the water sample spiked with standard NaClO solutions (10μL, final concentration 0–10μM) were diluted to 1mL with PBS containing TPA (the final concentration of TPA was 10μM). Then the PL intensities of the spiked solutions were measured.

The mass spectrum of the crystals exhibits a strong peak at m/z 241 (Fig. 1 a). The FTIR spectrum (Fig. S1) exhibits the absorption peaks of CC groups at around 1650cm−1, the peaks of CO group at around 1731cm−1, the peaks of COO− groups at 1528cm−1 and 1435cm−1, and the peaks of CO, CN and/or CS groups at around 1217cm−1. The results confirm the formation of TPA. As shown in Fig. 2 , the TPA solution shows a sharp absorption peak centered at around 345nm, and a strong emission peak with the maximum wavelength at around 415nm. The PL quantum yield of TPA is about 90.5%, calculated at the excitation of 345nm by selecting the quinine sulfate as the standard, and the calculation method is emerged in the supporting information. The results imply that TPA should be an excellent blue-emission phosphor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of the PL sensor for free chlorine based on TPA
ce:section.ce:para: The PL signal of TPA can be quenched sensitively by the free chlorine. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2, the TPA solution emits strong blue PL signal under the excitation of 365nm UV light, however, the signal is quenched obviously as 14μM free chlorine is added. The corresponding PL spectra indicate that the PL intensity is decreased by 85%, without any wavelength shift. On the contrary, the absorption peak blue shifts with slight decrease in intensity. The results imply that the free chlorine may react with TPA to produce some molecules with lower conjugation, which have nearly no PL activity. The free chlorine is a strong oxidation agent, while the sulfur atom in the thiazolo group of TPA can be oxidized [29]. Therefore, TPA may be oxidized by free chlorine. The mass spectrum of the oxidation product (Fig. 1b) also implies that the sulfur atom of TPA molecule may be oxidized into sulfoxide (Scheme 2 ), which is a strong electron withdrawing group rather than an electron donating group. As a result, the conjugation is decreased, which agrees well with the result from UV–vis absorption spectra. The decrease of conjugation leads to the great PL quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of the PL sensing for free chlorine
ce:section.ce:para: It has been mentioned that the phosphor, TPA, shows the maximum absorption at 345nm, and exhibited the maximum emission at 415nm. Therefore, the PL sensor was excitated at 345nm to measure the PL intensity at 415nm. Furthermore, the concentration of TPA is a pivotal factor for this sensing system. It has been discussed above that free chlorine quench the PL of TPA by reacting it into a poor luminescent product. In other words, the change of PL intensity is caused by the change of TPA concentration. Therefore, to obtain the best sensitivity of the sensor, the concentration of TPA should be controlled in a range where the PL intensity is sensitive to the change of concentration. As shown in Fig. S2, the PL intensity increases linearly as the concentration of TPA increases from 0 to 10μM. However, the increasing tendency slows down when the concentration of TPA is further increased. Accordingly, 10μM TPA is applied in the sensor.

Time-dependent PL changes of 10μM TPA upon the addition of 10μM NaClO are investigated. As shown in Fig. S3, the decrease tendency of PL intensity is very sharp in 10min, and turns to slow down when the reaction time is further increased. The PL intensity comes to the minimum at 20min, and keeps stable during the following 40min observation. The result indicates that the oxidation reaction between TPA and free chlorine is complete within 20min. Therefore, the PL signal of the sensor is measured after the NaClO is added into the solution for 20min.

The effects of pH value on the sensing system are subsequently discussed. As shown in Fig. 3 , the PL intensity of TPA is relative strong and stable in neutral and alkaline solutions, but decreases with the pH value in acidic solution. In particular, the PL intensity decreases obviously when the pH value is lower than 4. The pH effect on the PL intensity of TPA may be related to the protonation deprotonation of the carboxyl groups on the TPA molecules. The pH value affects not only the PL intensity of TPA, but also the quenching effect of free chlorine. It can be seen that the quenching efficiency (I0/I, where I0 and I are the PL intensity of TPA in the absence and presence of free chlorine, respectively) shows the maximum in pH 4, and decreases when the pH value is either increased or decreased. The results may be associated with the presence form of free chlorine [30]. It has been reported that free chlorine exists mainly as HClO when the pH value is around 4. However, it almost turns to Cl2 as the pH value is decreased to 3. In contrary, when the pH value is increased, it turns gradually to ClO−, whose oxidation capability is weaker than that of HClO. Therefore, the quenching efficiency decreases gradually with increasing pH value. In particular, when the pH value is higher than 8, free chlorine has nearly no effect on the PL intensity of TPA due to the fact that it presents mainly as ClO−. Accordingly, the sensing system should be carried out in the solution of pH 4.

To evaluate the selectivity of the sensing system for free chlorine, effects of 22 common ions (Br−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, CO3 2−, CH3CO2 −, PO4 3−, Ag+, Na+, K+, NH4 +, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Al3+) and 4 oxidative agents (ClO4 −, MnO4 −, Cr2O7 2−, H2O2) are investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 , none of the ions has detectable effect on the PL intensity of TPA even at a high concentration (all the ions are 100μM, except for MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2− are 10μM to avoid the interference from the color at high concentrations). Apparently, the sensor shows excellent selectivity towards free chlorine.

Under the optimized conditions mentioned above, the PL intensities of TPA upon adding a series concentration of free chlorine are measured. As shown in Fig. 5 , the PL intensity of TPA at 415nm decreases gradually as the concentration of TPA is increased. The quenching efficiency increases linearly with the concentration of free chlorine in the range from 0.2 to 14μM (see inset of Fig. 5 and Eq. (1)):
(1) (I0-I)/I0 =63673C+0.0019

The detection limit is calculated to be about 0.2μM. The free chlorine concentration in most water sample is in the range of 0.05–5mg/L (1.4–140μM). For example, world health organization (WHO, 1993) sets that the residual free chlorine in drinking water should be around 0.2–0.3mg/L (5.6–8.4μM) for 30min contact time [31]. The residual free chlorine concentration in water discharge from water treatment plants is typically below 5mg/L (140μM) [17]. Apparently, the response range and the detection limit of the sensor are sufficient to meet the necessary in the common detection of free chlorine. Actually, if necessary, the response range and the detection limit of the sensor can be adjusted by changing the concentration of TPA. For example, when the concentration of TPA is decreased to 0.3μM, the linearly response range is 0.02–0.8μM and the detection limit is calculated to be 8nM (Fig. S4).

The applicability of the developed sensor is further evaluated by detecting the concentration of free chlorine in a local tap water sample. In the experiment, the standard addition method was applied (Fig. S5). The free chlorine was measured to be 6.11±0.05μM (n=10, confidence level of 95%), which agrees well with the result using the DPD colorimetric method (6.06±0.17μM). The results confirm the validity of the TPA-based PL sensor for the detection of free chlorine in real water samples.

TPA molecule is synthesized by the condensation of CA and l-cysteine, and purified by crystallization. The purified TPA shows bright blue PL with a PLQY as high as 90.5%. It is found that the PL signal of TPA could be selectively quenched by the free chlorine, and the quenching mechanism is discussed. Based on the results, a sensitive and selective PL sensor of free chlorine is developed. Furthermore, using Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O to provide a red background PL signal, a visible PL ratiometric sensor can be build, which is convenient for the on-site and on-line detection of free chlorine. This study provides not only a facile method to synthesize high PL TPA, but also the sensing application of TPA.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675026, 21675027, 21375020), National Basic Research Program of China (2010CB732400), and the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT1116).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.01.070.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Beyond bottom-up carbon nanodots: citric-acid derived organic molecules
Luminescence phenomena of biodegradable photoluminescent poly(diol citrates)
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
Investigation from chemical structure to photoluminescent mechanism: a type of carbon dots from the pyrolysis of citric acid and an amine
Synthesis and photoluminescence properties of Π-extended fluorene derivatives: the first example of a fluorescent solvatochromic nitro-group-containing dye with a high fluorescence quantum yield
Influence of free residual chlorine on cultured human epidermal keratinocytes from normal skin and hypertrophic scars
Association between over-chlorinated drinking water and suckling, nursery and fattening pig death losses
Drinking water disinfection by-products: an Australian perspective
An improved NN′-diethyl-p-phenylenediamine (DPD) method for the determination of free chlorine based on multiple wavelength detection
Determination of chlorine in water by luminol chemiluminescence
Determination of hypochlorite in water using a chemiluminescent test strip
Chemiluminescence reaction of glucose-derived graphene quantum dots with hypochlorite, and its application to the determination of free chlorine
Carbon nitride quantum dots: a novel chemiluminescence system for selective detection of free chlorine in water
Electrochemical detection of free chlorine at highly boron-doped diamond electrodes
Electrochemical determination of a free chlorine residual using cathodic potential-step chronocoulometry
Electrochemical detection of free chlorine at inkjet printed silver electrodes
Determination of inorganic chlorine species in kraft mill bleach effluents by ion chromatography
Simultaneous determination of chlorine dioxide and hypochlorite in water by high-performance liquid chromatography
CdSe-ZnS quantum dots for selective and sensitive detection and quantification of hypochlorite
nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots: a facile and green fluorescence probe for free chlorine
A label-free fluorescent assay for free chlorine in drinking water based on protein-stabilized gold nanoclusters
Graphene quantum dot as a green and facile sensor for free chlorine in drinking water

Hydrothermal treatment of grass: a low-cost green route to nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Cu(II) ions
Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
Graphene oxide quantum dots@silver core?shell nanocrystals as turn-on fluorescent nanoprobe for ultrasensitive detection of prostate specific antigen
Novel turn-on fluorescent detection of alkaline phosphatase based on green synthesized carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets
Synthesis, crystal structure and oxidation of (R)-(+)-8, 9-dimethoxy-6, 10b-dihydro-5H-thiazolo[2, 3-a]isoquinolin-3-one
Continuous flow determination of free chlorine in water
Free chlorine residual content within the drinking water distribution systemInt

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Qingqing Fang received her BS (2014) from the College of Chemistry and Enviroment, Minna Normal University, China. She is currently pursuing her master’s degree under the supervision of Prof. Yongqiang at Fuzhou University, Fujian, China. She is actively engaged in novel synthesis of luminescent carbon based nanomaterials and their application in biosensing.

Qian Wang received her BS (2015) from the College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, China. She is currently pursuing her master’s degree under the supervision of Prof. Yongqiang at Fuzhou University, Fujian, China. Her research interest focus on the carbon based nanomaterials/metal based nanomaterials hybrids and their sensing applications.

Wei Lin received his BS (2014) from the College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, China. At present, he is studying for his doctorate (analytical chemistry) in Prof. YuWu Chi's group at Fuzhou University. The current field of interest is the synthesis and sensing application of carbon based catalysts.

Xu You received his BS (2014) from the department of chemistry, Hebei Normal University. At present, he is studying for his doctorate (analytical chemistry) in Prof. YuWu Chi's group at Fuzhou University. His research interests mainly focus on Electrochemiluminescent nanomaterial and biosensor.

Yongqiang Dong received his BS (2006) and PhD (2011) degrees from the department of chemistry, Fuzhou University, China. He entered Nanyang Technologic University, Singapore as a postdoctoral researcher from 2011 to 2013. Currently, he is an associate professor of chemistry at Fuzhou University. His research interests mainly relate to the synthesis, luminescent properties and sensing applications of carbon based dots.

Yuwu Chi received his BS (1992) and MS (2000, Prof. Guonan Chen) degrees in Analytical Chemistry at Fuzhou University, and then a Ph D degree in applied physics (2005, Prof. Koichi Aoki) from the University of Fukui. From 1992–2002, he worked at Fuzhou University as a research assistant and a lecturer in turn. In 2005, he re-entered Fuzhou University after obtaining his Ph D degree in Japan, and worked as a professor at the College of Chemistry. Presently, his research interests focus on functional carbon materials, electrochemiluminescence, electroanalysis, chemical and biochemical sensing.
